humanities_role_explanation: Humanities topics (such as art, history, TV shows, and movies) are treated purely as a source of complex, multi-constraint factual data. The benchmark leverages these domains to construct highly obscure, reverse-engineered trivia questions to test agentic search capabilities, rather than evaluating interpretive, aesthetic, or critical humanistic methodology.
benchmark_modalities_explanation: The benchmark currently consists entirely of natural language questions and short textual reference answers.
analytical_tasks_explanation: Tasks require the agent to parse complex, multi-hop factual constraints (e.g., matching a fictional character to specific backstory, television, and episode-count criteria) and locate a single, verifiable, short-string answer across the open web.

general_purpose_benchmark.note: It is designed as a general-purpose capability benchmark for web-browsing agents across multiple knowledge domains.

evaluates_generative_llm.note: Evaluates frontier generative and reasoning models including GPT-4o, GPT-4.5, OpenAI o1, and OpenAI Deep Research.

llm_is_primary_system.note: The primary systems being benchmarked are LLM-based web-browsing agents.

arts_humanities_data.note: Includes substantial factual content from History, Visual Arts and Performing Arts (TV, movies, art), Music, and Literature (fictional characters).

quantitative_metrics.note: Evaluates models using accuracy (percentage of matches verified by an LLM-as-a-judge) and calibration error.

published_after_march_2023.note: The preprint was published in April 2025.

english_language.note: The paper and the benchmark dataset are in English.

summary: BrowseComp is a benchmark designed to evaluate the capability of AI agents to persistently browse the web to resolve complex, hard-to-find questions. It features 1,266 human-curated, multi-constraint queries that are difficult to solve but simple to verify. The evaluation demonstrates that while standard LLMs fail, agentic systems with test-time compute can achieve over 50% accuracy through strategic browsing.
key_findings: Standard models without browsing (GPT-4o, GPT-4.5) achieve less than 1% accuracy, while OpenAI o1 (using internal reasoning without search) reaches 9.9%. The agentic 'Deep Research' model achieves 51.5% accuracy, which scales smoothly with increased test-time compute and can be further boosted by 15% to 25% using best-of-N voting strategies. Human trainers only solved 29.2% of the tasks within a two-hour limit, illustrating the dataset's high difficulty.
